speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: I came over here to Washington, home city out here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know how we came to locate around here?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: I don't know, just like everybody else. I heard about the land, the pretty things about every home we've said. I don't know. I never heard him say.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He took the place and then he went out to work? Is that it?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Yeah, he bought them the home city and went out over here where a Pullman worked.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Yeah, I was born over there, in Pullman.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What can I call it, eh? But you grew it up right around here, right?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about here?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You want to tell me again about Marshall Hayes and the story of the shooting, the way you remember it, the way you heard about it?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Oh, that was out of town at the time it happened. They went over there to arrest a sly and the sly told him never to come and arrest him. He'd kill him if he did, so I said. So he went up there and he shot him through the neck, cut his neck tile. Then he started running down the hills. Bill Fogg was from Guggen, took him to buy a tile pile and there's where he really died there. He was unloading toys. And then the sly he kept shooting them down the town. Hayes and the boys, they get over there towards where the depot was there. The water tank used to be there. Every time they'd shoot at them, they'd walk down the hillside and there was a folks' place there and they arrested him. Took him to Moscow.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Now, about them taking him to Moscow, you told me that some of these townspeople got really accurate. Oh, God, yeah, well, they talked that about and they go out and go to rob him. They wanted to kill him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They want to lynch him?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Yeah, some of them wanted to lynch him. Then my daddy told him it was bullish. That's when they all take care of the facts. So the sheriff was down there and they had their team in the liver barn and they took the team out of the liver barn and back through the back door, back out in and got him in the sleigh and the corner and he went up to the, probably where the brickyard is now, under the overpass, the railroad there. Then he got out and come back down to town and there was a freight train come up there about ten o 'clock along the river. There was a freight train that had to take the engineers back in and go up and had him off.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had him off before he got to Moscow?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Before he got to Moscow, yeah. But the train was late. After eleven o 'clock when they come in, he had the time to get him and the sheriff told him to take him fast so he could go. He'd be sure to get him up there. That's what they done.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Told him to take him fast?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Yeah, drive him fast as a horse and stand him. So that's the way he took him up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know who was in there in the wagon there with him? Who took him in?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Oh, the deputy sheriff, that's all I know. I don't know what the sheriff's name was. Now I don't remember.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, why were these guys mad at Sly?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: I thought this Hayes fellow was pretty unpopular. I thought they didn't like Hayes very much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why were they mad at Sly?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Well, I thought Joe Manhays was around the odd fella lodge and some of them called you the marshal. They respected him.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Yeah, he just wasn't like me very much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why was it the people didn't like him?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Well, because he was overbearing I guess. He'd rat people, didn't do hardly anything and he'd rat them anyway.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: I've heard his sons didn't have a very good reputation either. Is that... Well, one of them was a farmer. And the other one, I don't know what he did. He just worked whatever job they come around I guess.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Yes. Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, when they came to get the wagon, you saw that?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: You saw... No, I saw them. See them, they took the cutter out and put the thing in and they carried him up. The deputy and the sheriff had him and they set him on their hands. Had their hands locked and carried him up. Took him out of the back steps of the hotel. Had him upstairs in the hotel and took him out, back stepped him up. And then I was there at the delivery table and they took the team out. Set him in there and took him. Did you ever hear that Mrs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Hayes swore she was going to get sly?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: No. I've heard that once, I don't know if it's true.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Let me ask you about that other one that you told me some about. That was how Driscoll got stabbed that time in Troy. Well, Driscoll was up, they had a dance up at Rosebud Hall. And he said, Driscoll, yes, just William Compton girls take her home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Then she turned him down and when they were going down the street by this shoemaker he said to all them other girls there, I said, sister, you never made a date, didn't you?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: And this Driscoll, they asked him if it was any Hayes business. And they went down the walkway, finally nice.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you say? They what?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: And he said Driscoll was the one that saw him and asked if the shoemaker was any Hayes business. Yeah. And they went on down the street a little ways. So he used to call the Hayes building down there, about where the page service station was there. There was a guy's ark in there and he up and diced him. And he went down to the drug store, the drug store. And it was locked up and they got the doctor down there and took him up to the doctor's office. And they kept him. He said he was just thinking what they could do for him. His arteries were all cut off.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Arteries were cut off?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Yeah, just cutting him out across the stomach. Talk as loud as you can, daddy, your voice is so low anyway. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what happened? Did he, did he took him a while to die?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: He diced about 1130 I think it was, along there. It was about seven o 'clock I think it was, next morning when he died. With the doctor couldn't stop bleeding. Too many arteries cut.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was right here in Troy he died?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Yes. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now what happened after that to Shoemaker?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Oh, he, he started to go about to work. He was working on the section and he started to go to work. And Doc Woodard was a marshal so they was watching him just during the night. They suspected him and when they got on there they arrested him. And brought him back up to town. And his wife didn't know anything about it I guess. The train was coming in to take him to Moscow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did she find out about it then?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Somebody told her. The train was just about to pull in when she found it out. And he took him, sent him up for 20 years. And his daddy was a feature leg of his father. He was working on, he'd been working on section and section more than quite a while. And he was taking care of the family and they moved over to Orofino. And there was the last ever I knew of him. And over there. And he kind of got pardoned out after, I don't know, it was probably about five years or something that way. Then they come home and his wife was getting gray -headed. Said he was ashamed of her because she couldn't gray hair. And he left and said he was somewhere up around Spokane when he died.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: That's just hard to believe that he'd say that. Yeah. You know. Yeah. Yes, but Pete Shoemaker was a truthful man and always was. And a good Christian man that said it. So he left his wife. Yeah, he just went out and left the whole family.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well now, did anybody, any of the Driscolls try to get revenge for this guy getting killed?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Well, this Driscoll had a brother here, but he described Mr. Shoemaker to the authorities. That's how they have to watch him. He said the fella was doing it while wearing a brown suit. Just guys about his height. They went and watched him. He didn't get away that night. So nobody saw the murder. They just figured he did it. They didn't see him stabbed. Well, some of them fellas, as long as it was dark, they'd walk along together. And he just said he's there watching me knife me until that's all he could do.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: That sounds like a stupid murder if I ever heard one. Yeah, yeah. Yeah. Yeah, I guess he told my mother he was in the Philippine War. And I think he told her he was like shoot them fellas just to see them kick. Yes. He was a pretty hard -hearted man like that. A shoemaker. Yeah. I'll be.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: I'll be. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever hear about Clem getting killed in Troy?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Yeah, he was killed here in Troy. I think it was a Negro running around. Killed him I think it was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A what?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: A Negro. Negro. Yes, you're right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened there?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: I don't know what happened. He just went down there and don't know what happened anyway. He killed him.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Was it Clem or was it Clem and Hagen daddy that got killed?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: I always thought it was Clem and Hagen. No, it was Clem.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: It was a brother that shot his Clem.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: They both lived on the ridge. Clem and Hagen and Clem both.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know anything that happened to the Negro after that?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: No, I don't know what happened to him. I forgot about it. I just have heard that from one or two folks that that had happened here in town. Clem's out here on the ridge could tell you that. They had a restaurant that used to be on that way to go by the hotel going towards the depot. And they had a restaurant down there. Used to be to have a water hole vacuum in there. And they had a restaurant built up in there. And then they filled that all in there afterwards. And he went down there. I don't know what happened.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: He was killed down there anyway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, speaking of Negroes, you told me you knew Joe Wells, right? What? You told me you knew Joe Wells, right?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Yeah. He was there. Joe and Chuck and his boy Roy and Chuck Wells and the other girls. Joe, he used to stop at the folk house. That's when they first come up the country there. They come through and come to Troy and go into Moscow. They'd stop.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was right on the way to Troy, your folks' place?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Right on the road from Deary?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Yeah, it's a couple of miles. Richard Spencer lives there now. The house there, up and around the turns. The house back off the road there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, didn't you tell me you played a trick on Joe Wells?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Well, when I was a kid, a little kid, my mother told him not to say anything. He was a nationally black man. So I was sauntering around there, kid -like, you know. I said to him, you better go wash your face. Take on old Joe. He said, he thinks I've been rooting against the black log.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Yeah, there's old Joe. You know, that's when old man Hayes was marshaled down here. Him and a fellow named Gunnarsson lived up there with Deary. They was in town here and there was horseback. They started homing and Joe, he started singing when he was going up out in town. And old man Hayes took in after him and he caught all those men, Gunnarsson. But he didn't catch Joe. So Joe had a little lone horse and he slapped Roney on the neck and told him, take this coon out of here, he said. So that's the way he done it. He didn't come back to town for a month or two. Yeah, come back and he's laughing about it. And he take this coon out of here, he said.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: And it's really true that people didn't care, people didn't care that he was a black man, Joe Wells. No, he mingled with all of us white people and everybody thought well of him. No, I... Good guy, no kidding. And his family was good people. There was two fellas that lived out here by the Yule Pass, about the divide rather, that house on this side of the divide. And their name was Wells and Deary was from back east there, wherever he was. And they come out here and homesteaded. And he come out after they did and homesteaded up there by Deary. And he go out there once in a while. I think his other father had him as a slave. And he was, they come out here and homesteaded.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they close? Was he close to the other, to the White Wells boys?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: He was up here at Deary.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what I mean is did they get, were they close friends?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Oh yeah, yeah. Yeah, he followed them out here and then they took the homestead up there at Deary. And he go back and forth, messaged with them.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Well, one thing I really wanted to ask you both was what it was like to be growing up on a homestead.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You grew up on a homestead, right? Did you get that dryer that I put the piece in the paper?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: No, I don't think I did. Well, my sister wrote the poem in 64. And she sent it to me for Christmas of our life on the homestead. And I asked for the writing back when they printed it for me.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: 1900 was pretty late to be homesteading around here, I would have thought. Well, it was, but I don't know how come that the land was thrown open. My daddy was working in Washington when we heard about it. And we came up here in 1898.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: And he worked in the woods. That's all there was to do, saw logs and skidding logs and things like that until this land opened. And then when it opened there were several others filed a homestead. But he bought this man's lease and used his right for our 160 that he got. But anyway he filed in 1900, same as the other guys.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where is this now from Troy?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: It's right straight north upon this mountain, two miles from the top down.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is it a bean farm now?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Oh, there's no farms up in there. Now it's just timber.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yes, and the fire went through in 1930, wasn't it?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Well, they cleared up some of the... And the timber and brush, of course, were screwed up since then. But they said it took all of the buildings off of our homestead except the log barn, it was still standing. My brother's been up there, but I never have, since the fire, I mean.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now did he go about starting from scratch? Did he have to build his own house on the place?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: No, there was a house. And he built a well, it was a house, and one acre of ground cleared where the orchard and our berries and our fruits of all kinds were here. And our garden was inside of a homemade picket fence. This fella that he bought the lease of, cleared that acre, built that house and barn, and put that picket fence around it. Then his wife took sick, and he had to do something, and he didn't want to lose his right, and what money he had in it. So he sold it to my daddy. I think it was $500 for his work. Now I'm not sure about that, but I think it was when my daddy fired on me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the house like? How big was it?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Well, I just don't know how big it was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I mean, were there many rooms in it?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: There wasn't any rooms in it. It was all in one when we bought it. Daddy made two bedrooms in it after we got there. But our front room and kitchen was clear across the front part together.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: And then there was two of us older ones that slept up in the loft till we got the other part of the house built. He built onto it later on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you work around the house very much when you were a kid? Were you expected to help out a lot?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: I guess I did. This girl that wrote this poem was born when I was 12 years old. The ninth, the man, she was born the tenth of May. And from then on I did help Daddy with the washing, and I did all of the cooking and all of the baking.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You did it all?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: I did. If my Daddy was living, he'd tell you the same. And I've been cooking for the whole family ever since. And they make my place right here now, just can tell you the same. All of my brothers and sisters that's living, well his too, if they want to come, they make this their home. My brothers is just real close. They all come, these three of them lives right here in town. And they all come here when they get into a pickle or they want to know something, or way back when they come to Mabel. And of course my sister lives now in Hartline, she's far away. When we were married, my mother was very poorly when she was born. So she was so little that they couldn't dress her on the table. So they dressed her on a pillow across their knees.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your mother?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: No, my sister.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: And my Daddy come along and he picked up her hand when the lady was dressing her and he said, Well what are we going to call her?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: And this Mrs. Meeker that was taking care of her, she looked up at him and she said, Well there's no other name but Dolly. She's just like a little dolly, and she was. She was just pretty as she was being, she was so little and so fat. So she's called Dolly, she don't know any other name in her own family here but Dolly. But her name is Laura Bell, her real name.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well was your mother not very well after this?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: No, she had to stay in bed. Dad and I did all the work. He helped me with the washings because we had to wash, of course, on the board. Carry the water from a spring about an eighth of a mile maybe from the house. And I couldn't do it all at twelve years old. And I've got one brother that's a year younger but he couldn't help me much with housework. He really helped outside though. And my sister, my next oldest sister was three years younger. She helped me with the dishes, but the cooking and all that work I did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I just, now you were how old, twelve years old?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Twelve years old.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Then we moved off of there in nineteen -eight. I'd really like to know a little more about what it meant for a kid to be having to do that kind of work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For cooking, where did you get your know how to do all the cooking from?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: My mother said from the time I could stand on a chair, and I know it was so, I had my nose and everything she made. And now I took her down. That was so. And my mother was a good cook. She was from the east and her daddy was a German. And she mixed up some of the best German meals I ever ate. And I just stood there and took it all in. And for baking, you know you couldn't buy a loaf of bread then. You couldn't buy a pie. You couldn't buy nothing. You had to make it or do without. So my mother had an east starter in a half a gallon jar. And I watched her and I know just exactly how she mixed that east up every time she baked out of it. And I made her two. And I baked her that east about 20 years afterward. Maybe longer. I think I baked some after Jess and me was married. Same east. Same kind of east. Yes sir. And his sister that lived in Spokane still baked out of it only about four years ago. She's been dead four years. And that was the same kind of east starter.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have to work all day to take care of a family this size?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Yes sir. I hardly sat down. And yet, I'm an awful light sleeper yet. And if anything went wrong with the family, dad always hollered for me. No matter what time of night or morning or anything else. And I always got up and helped you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would happen to the family? You mean some of the kids?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Well, some of the rest of them would get sick. Or if he couldn't find what he wanted to get in. We had to be our own doctor for it near then. Our nearest doctor then was in Moscow. They got one later on in the years before we moved off there out here at Joel. Old Dr. Hunt.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were the kinds of sicknesses that folks would be likely to get then?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Well, they'd be flu. They was what the old fashioned people used to call a grip. And they'd get that every winter. Some of them had real bad tonsils. They had tonsillitis. They was almost everything like all bunches of kids had. But we was lucky until... Oh, I think it was the next spring. Dolly was about a year old. Dad went to Moscow with a load of wood from off of the mountain. And he took it up to his doctor that had been coming clear from Moscow. A doctor of the family when they had to have doctors. And when he come home, about three or four days afterwards, he got awful sick. Oh, he was just deathly sick. And we couldn't get him to bed at first. He laid on the floor on the pallet and we got him to bed. And he began to get better of his sickness of the stomach, but he began to break out. And he had scarlet fever. So we called the doctor. And of course we all had it. It went clear through the family then. So that old doctor there in Moscow would come out and he'd come as far as he could. And when he couldn't get all the way, some of the neighbors would go eat him and bring him up. But nobody dared come in. We didn't dare go out because of quarantine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long did that scarlet fever take?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: It took us six weeks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it like?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: My brother John didn't take it. He was immune to it, the doctor said. So the other four of us all had it. Mama didn't because she'd had it. When she was a girl at home, she had scarlet fever. Then the next winter, my brother Don, the fourth one, took a fever of some kind. And the doctor said it wasn't mountain fever, it wasn't rheumatic fever, it wasn't typhoid.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: And now I forgot the name of the fever it was. But anyway, he was three years old. And he had to learn to walk over again after that fever was... And he kept wanting a little red pair of boots. And my daddy told him, he said, well, son, he said, whenever you can walk, you get them little red boots. And he'd try until he just swept and he couldn't walk. He couldn't get up and stand or he couldn't walk. But finally, after weeks, I don't know how long, why he did get so he could walk. But it sure did affect his walking man.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have any remedy for a way to treat scarlet fever? Was there any prescription?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: No, nothing, only the doctor gave us a diet, more or less, with no animal fats much to keep the fever out. And lots of teas. We had to drink sass, fresh tea. And there was another kind he gave us. See, there was no made juices then. You couldn't get juices. All you could get was the lemons and the oranges and things like that to make your own. And I think that other kind of tea he gave us.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about for some of the other sicknesses, like for the flu? Was there any treatment for flu, any home remedies?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Well, nothing, only just our own home remedies, like I told you in this year before.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were they? What was the remedy you would use?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: For lungs and throat. Why, you'd take this goose grease and camphor gum and turpentine, equal parts. Keep them real warm and massage them with that every three hours, back and front, till it was broken. That worked pretty good, you bet that worked. And another, well, years later we used that, was fried onion polis. We broke that on my youngest brother's when he was just a little fellow. Broke pneumonia with fried onion polis.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's that mean? You just fried the onions?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: You just fry the onions and put them in a sack, like a piece of flour sack or muslin, whatever you had to make a small sack of. I would go over the lungs. Put one in front of them, one in the back, and keep them good and warm, and keep them onions warm. We had to keep them on the stove, of course, to keep them warm.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you ever up at the Cooley Dam? Have you ever been up there?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Yes, I have been. Out there, all that lake, the Cooley there, that used to all be farmland. The government took that, bought that all up. That's the retaining lake. Tell him how far your place was from there, Daddy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, that was 20 miles from Cooley City, and up there was T - How did you ever find out about that?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: I don't know, I lived up there, some of us. I was up there in home city, and lived there. The government took it over.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: What year did you file, Daddy?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Uh, 1908, I think it was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how did you make do on that place? Was there anything there when you got there?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: No, no, no. I was just a land. I built a shack, a house, and got out about 40 acres. I worked for ranches around the country there. Lots of cattle. You worked on cattle ranches.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there many people around you?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: No, there were people all around there. Lots of people lived there. No, I was - people had, oh, I'm putting here about 160 acres, I guess. You proved up on it. You stayed there. Yeah, yeah, I proved up on it, and then the government took it over and made the lake out of it. He had 320 acres.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how did you get the other 160?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: I got additional homesteads. I got 320 out of it, out of the way. And there was other people there, bought up other people's rights.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, there was mostly - a lot of it was cattle ranching there?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Well, it used to be cattle. In those days, cattle was about all the way there, but then they got to plowing it up, and they made wheat and rye and stuff like that.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: My uncle had a - was on a irrigated ranch up there. He was up in Polk Island, he was. He got, you know,

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: I don't know what the fella's name was, but he owned it. He rented a place of them up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the nearest town to you? What was the nearest town to where you were?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Coolest any. 20 miles. 20 miles.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: You know, that there, all the - that retaining lake in Coolest City, north of the river. That's when they irrigate the whole country. And that's where they cool your dangly at.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there a lot of rattlesnakes in that country?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Quite a few, yeah. Some places you find quite a lot of them, then you go and you wouldn't find them for quite a while then. They stayed where there was rock here mostly. And, yeah.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: How many days was it we put in harvest there, Daddy?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: One, it was 50 days we put in the size machine. I cooked and he fired the engine. I worked the hay in and then the head with the header, cutting the grain, then the trash machine. I put in 84 days altogether. Heading and hay in and harvesting the head and the grain, then the trash machine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it somebody else's machine or was it yours?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: No, no, it was blowing another fire.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now how did the crops grow there? It was pretty good?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Oh, not like they did here. They don't really show the grain, so you hit it good. It raised pretty good, yield pretty good, but it was probably 30 foot long in there. Not on the, cut from the high ground out of the coulee. But it started 40 foot long in the acre there, full of wheat.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: But down there in the coulee is a little about, the grain got about 18, 20 inches high, something like that. But it was still there.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Not near like it did on top.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now you sort of wanted to stay out there? Is that it?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Yes, I wanted to stay there and me and him buy up some cattle.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The cattle was so pretty and the land that was irrigated when we was married and went up there and harvested, how many crops did they get daddy?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Three crops. Yes, of alfalfa. And I never saw such pretty herds of cattle in all of my life. You could, they supposed to go right down their back with your hand. Bus grass, all that kind of, in those days, when I went up there, there was a home center, plowing out of it. They said, putting your knee high. And the cattle buyers come in there and you never see no fat cattle. See, well, they just decided to work in and out to some fat. Well, they'd walk. That bush grass was about that high. It was still pretty good for cattle raising when you were out there. Oh, yes. And Ed's Rock, he had lots of cattle and they got homesteading and he moved up to Okanagan. And he had his head herdsman, his name was Jim Drew, said he was paying on 700 head pastures. And he said he had a near solid head that did 700. And I've seen him drive out of Canada, he'd bring them down through and bring them down to Kuna City and ship them. And I see him come down there and they'd drive about 10, 12 miles a day, just let them just walk along. And take them down there and all the two engines would pull up out of Kuna. They had a small engine, but they'd have several carloads.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Tell them about, who was you working for that winter that you skinned so many cattle that evening?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Well, I was laying in Jamestown and I was in his cattle and they let them stay out and didn't draw them up.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: So late, they had the calves as big as the cows was, wasn't they?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: And they just had them nurse down and they just carved out and they'd drive them, couldn't feed them up enough. And then we had to skin cattle, get the hides off of them. We'd take them, skin their legs down a little and then pitch the team onto the head of the, well, skin the back of the head a little bit, you know. Take a hog chain and put it around the hide and just pull it off of the team. That's what I wanted to tell you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What time of the year was this? When was this during the year? What time of the year?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Winter time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was right in the middle winter?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Yeah. Yeah, they'll make good quality feed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Make good feed?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Yeah, when they've drunk the cattle off, you know, they just cows would eat them up. It sure must have sounded funny, the noise it made pulling off them hides. Yeah, it sure made good clean hides. That's all the profit they had was the hides, I guess. They just couldn't take the meat at all. Well,

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: that was a miserable job.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: He won't eat meat much and he won't eat butter. Tell him why he won't eat butter and milk. I worked up this, this creamery.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: At the university?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: University. I was eating, they had cows up there, I had gorgets.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had what?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Had gorgets.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Gorgets in the bag?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: In the bag.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And milking?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: And then there's stringy milk. And they'd take that stuff out of there and then they'd take that up to the university. It's a creamery there. I don't want to go with it up there. Probably made it into butter. Butter and cheese and stuff. And we got all that stuff free when he was working there that we could use. Cottage cheese and butter, buttermilk, anything we wanted.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But he didn't like, it's that bad, eh?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: It's really bad stuff. To this day you can't get in to eat a bite of butter. He won't drink a drop of milk or nothing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What is the garden?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: It's a bag disease that gets in the bag of the cow. I don't know. Some of the blame is from laying on wet ground or something like that. Yeah, when they get fever in the bag. It gets in there and there's just stringy milk. Just hardens and comes out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, why did you, so why did you pick up and come back here and leave that country over there?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: He wouldn't stay there. He rented a ranch before we went up there. Out here, the other side of these mothers, going toward the lake.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were tired of that country?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Yeah. You said he'd end up there twelve years alone and he wasn't living there any longer. I was too far from town. And he'd come down here. We'd come down with a pocket full of money. We both had all of our harvest wages. And I never saw such vegetables and fruit growing in the country than there was up there. And our neighbors just gave us all we could use. And I made twelve gallons of green cucumber pickles. Oh, they were good. Great big green tomatoes. And I canned them in an old fashioned stone jar. And I brought them jars down here with us. All twelve of them full of pickles.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: And we came down here with all of our money, a buggy, and the seven head, wasn't it seven head horses, Daddy?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: I think so. And started farming. But he rented the farm before we left here. We left here to go to Cooley City to harvest the tenth of August. After we was married the twentieth of June. And we didn't, well we had a few days up there. He hitched up his driving team. He had one of the best driving teams I ever pulled a line over. And he would take me all over the country after we went up where we was going to work. And seen about our job. And we, I'd fixed enough lunch for our dinner and our supper. If we'd be out where we couldn't get nothing to eat. And our drinks. We both drank water. And we'd start out just as quick as we got up and we wouldn't get home till after dark. For days on top of days till we went to thrashing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you looking the country over?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Yes, he's taken me all over the country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did you go? Where did he take you? What did you see?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Well, I seen everything, I think. And rattlesnakes to boot. And I was just scared to death of them. One morning we got up and I put the washing out. You had to put the washing out early. Get it dry. If you didn't dry sandstorm, I'd just whip it to pieces. And it was dirty again. I got up and washed and scrubbed the floor. His cabin was all in one room but it was nice. It was big enough that he'd locked the room. Scrubbed the floor. I started out after a bucket of water at the well right at the side of the house. Had a pitcher of pump in it. And I looked out and as I was going out the door I got about half way to the well. I met a great big snake. Oh God, I went running back in the house. He had side windows you could slide in. And I opened one of them windows and Lordy, I let a hoop out of me for him to come quick and get his rattlesnake out there. And he said it was a bull snake. But oh, it was a big one. Those bull snakes are... Yes, and I stood on the bed when I hollered at him too. Foot of the bed. Oh, I was afraid of snakes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you remember about that harvest?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: You worked real hard I imagine in the kitchen. Oh boy, and it was 110 every day we was in the cook house. And I don't know how many men we had.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Do you know, Daddy? How many men there were? In the harvest?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: About 20. And the old lady I was cooking with was too old to cook. And the bosses they didn't like it, but they had had her so long they didn't want to turn her down so they said they'd try her.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: And after we was out a week or two one of the bosses come to me and he said, will you take over the first cook?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: And I said yes, any day. And I said Mrs. Patrick's a good woman and she's just too old for this job. Well he said if you take over first cook, I'll be willing to pay you more. And I said I don't want any more than she gets. She's a good worker and a good woman. So I went to cooking and Bill come in one day and I was frying donuts. And the table that he ate at was right in the cook stove, close to that wood box that was there. And he sat down at the table and he started, well he went first and got him a cup of coffee, it's always made on the back of the stove. And got him a cup of coffee and he sat down and he started eating donuts. Well this Mrs. Patrick's husband was roughed about for the cook house and the machine. And he come in and he also got him a cup of coffee and started eating warm donuts. Nobody said anything because Bill was one of the bosses and he loved to eat and he liked good cooking. So I kept frying donuts and they kept eating and finally old Mr. Patrick spoke up and he said, well Bill, I'll tell you one thing. This cooked as a cooking now, you better keep your elbow out of the shortening can. And that didn't strike Bill a bit and he said, well I don't know about that.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: He said, what's wrong?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: He said, there ain't anything wrong. He said, this is just the way I like it and don't say no more about it. And we went all through harvest. We never had a word. Nobody. But that old lady was just too old to do that much managing and cooking. And she done the peeling vegetables and helped with the dishes and picking up and things like that. I done the scrubbing, I done the washing, we washed every day and I'd scrub the kitchen every day and let her go lay down after dinner. Because if I hadn't, if she couldn't have held out. But we put in better than 50, I got 50 and daddy got 51. Days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that the first time you'd cooked for a harvest crew?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Oh Lord no. Cooked for my daddy for seven years. He owned a machine. Before that and then I've cooked since him and me's been married. I've cooked all over the ridges out here for the machine men when they had a machine. And one of them was Ed Ramsdale. Cooked for him two years in the harvest. Well them guys are the best tracking team I ever worked for. Boy they were, they were just... They never had a job. Every man had a job on the move. He was supposed to do that job and take care of it. And if they didn't do what they ought to do, they didn't argue with it. They just kept them checked and it's almost to it. And they averaged on a movement from 8 to 10 minutes. From one setting to another. Every man had a job to do. That is fast. Yeah. We just had high fire engines and that. We'd start in the morning and they didn't start early like the machine does. And by night we'd be through supper. You'd hear whistles all over the country while they were working. They worked about ten hours about as long as they worked.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really? You mean you didn't work from dawn till dark?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: No. We'd go have breakfast from morning on. It was around six o 'clock. Then we'd start up the machine around till noon and have supper.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I mean what time was it?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: About six o 'clock. About six o 'clock.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And you got as much done as the other outfits did?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: We made better averages. Yes. And we were running there. We were right there. I forget where I was now. I don't know how many days it was. But we had a 1316 track. Well it started two inch machine. So you know they were putting the grain through pretty fast. And that was the chicken dinner. And the boys would get the chickens and then they'd take them to the engine and scald them and pick them and everything for us. Every time they got that many sacks or over was chicken dinner. Fried chicken dinner. Well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You sure?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Never mind. I had a job. That's all you were supposed to take care of. I've heard that the cooks had the hardest jobs of all on the crew because they had to work so long. They put in the longest hours probably. But I don't know. I tell you, you work in the hot sun at 110 out there. Them men really did work now. And they worked hard.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get enough sleep when you were on a job like that?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Oh yes. Like I told you I did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why do you think that was? You think the lemonade would be better than water?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Oh yes. Yeah. The lemons. Make it so you didn't get diarrhea or anything like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that the only?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: That's different. It wasn't often that you'd have lemonade. Most crews it would be water. Yeah. You see they changed water.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: And there was so much alkaline, that water up there. Oh it was terrible some places. On top of the high ground it wasn't alkaline. And it could have used alkaline water.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have trouble getting water on your homestead?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: No. He had one of the finest springs right around his house that ever flowed. There was a spring around there that, around the year round. There was one spring there on the Tom Hargeson place. That's the courtner for his brother said he'd been watching for 35 years. And it never changed. And there was a white streak about that wide. About that, about that far down on the rock in that spring. You'd go there in the wintertime. You'd run in the sand. You'd go in there and dive into a little lake. And then you'd go there in the hottest days of the summer. You'd run in just the sand. You'd come out from under a kind of hill. And you'd run out of the year round. And up there's all the net, under that water, that storage lake. And I was just wondering what happened to them springs. That storage lake all over them there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember how you found the homestead that you took up? How you found it? Did you have a locator?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Oh, I had it. It was all surveyed out. And then there was land taken up around it. Just had bacon down there. I fired on it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ask around and find out where there was more, where there was open ground?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: I found my uncle, a fellow named Morton there. Billy Morton lived there. He knew about it. Got the bacon down there. So I just went and fired on it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How do you think that country compares with this country?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Oh, it ain't, you know, where a deer like this is, a farmer. They come up on the ground, on the high ground there. They raise pretty good crops.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have trouble getting timber, getting wood to build your house?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Oh no. I got lumber. I was buying lumber. They shipped lumber in.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: There was trees over in the granite, but there wasn't any trees on them. I had one tree on them, a big pine tree on the place I lived on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's all the trees there was?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: It was about, I bet, 50 feet under them big limbs, spread out. It was your big bunch of trees.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now when you were a boy living here, was it your aunt that you spent a lot of time with? Who was the woman that you spent a lot of time with? Was that your aunt or your grandmother?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Oh, grandmother. I spent quite a lot of time with her. She had a home set up on the Moscow mountain. She stayed up there with her quite a bit.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Well, from what you were saying before, it sounded like she was quite a special woman, and I thought maybe you'd tell me a little bit about her. Well, sure. Really, I didn't know much about her. She didn't live too long after we was married. But I know quite a bit about her when she's on the mountain because we used to go by her place and stop.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: She was a wonderful woman. She could do anything. Chop wood, saw logs. Well, I can too. But she did it alone up there for so many years on that place. I don't see how she did it. Then she had a little band of sheep to take care of. And they just looked beautiful. She'd heard them all day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she spin her own wool when she was here in this country?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Yes, out to Jess's dad, she did. I saw her and grandma, his mother, spin some wool on the cardan. They carded it first on cardan boards. They have little handles like a, well I called them a curry comb, like they use for a horse. They'd take cardan, they'd take rollin', makein' a roll, then you'd take a spinnin' wheel. You'd turn that and the needle sticks out and you just, till it rhymes enough to make the yarn out of it. Just keeps it goin', and you keep windin' it on. There's one or two, used to be two spinning wheels here in town yet. Anna Marie Anderson's got one up on her homestead, or her daddy's homestead here. And then Lena Olson used to have one, but some couple of quacks came in and beat her out of her spinning wheel. Took it and never paid her for it. She was alone and a little bit of an old lady. She's puttin' her a hundred years old and still livin'.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she make the wool your grandmother out of her, from her own sheep?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: She'd cheer who she'd do. Yeah, they'd take a wash of wool and card it. That's what she made that bedspread out of, I showed you. Her own wool. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She had to do everything for herself on the homestead, right?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Oh yes. Well, only what the boys helped her. Sometimes Jess's brother would go and stay a little while. Well, he had two brothers that would go, and Jess would go and stay a little while with her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And she was right near your home place?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: No, she wasn't near. She was clear across the canyon. Oh, I see. We had to go down and around to get to her place. But it wasn't very far, right straight through, but right across the canyon.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you say that she made her, uh, uh, she made cedar, cedar shakes?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Yes. She made shakes. Cause I seen her. I don't know. I think Jess has got her, her fro or his daddy's fro out here yet. Uh, make shakes with.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Which one was it, daddy? Grandma's fro that she made shakes or your dad's?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: I don't know. One's my dad's. I don't know who's his brother. Well, it's grandma's then. She's all the two that had one. It just sounds to me like a, from what you were saying now before, like she's a very independent woman. Yes. And boy did she have a temper. If she told you no, you better take no. No, that's like when we went working down here at the minkeler. We used crows and a mallet to make these pickets. Then they got them splitting machines a long time afterwards. And then they picked on them for quite a while.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Was a pro.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Well, I was going to ask you for the story about how, how you first met, uh, Mr. Spencer. Now you met him the second time too. Yes. My egg lady is coming. And I'll empty it so she can take her cartons back. Got to get some puffer puffer to pay her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Tell me the story now about how, how you first met this husband of yours?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: First time I seen him, he made me mad. He pulled up my hair. And I jumped over the teeter board and took after him. And of course he'd run. And I told him if he's the last man on earth, I wouldn't have him. And now he's pretty good sized kids. Both of us. He's staying with his grandmother. They didn't go to school. And then I didn't meet him anymore until 1914. Well, it was the year, first part of the year of 15.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: When is Bill's birthday, daddy?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: I don't know. I met him at his brother's birthday party anyway. And I asked Frank Rowan. He's the guy I went there with.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, you were there with Frank Rowan?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Well, we've known him ever since he came to this country. And he lived out there then. On the same road.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: I said to Frank, he's eating supper. He's sitting on an old trunk.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I said, who is that guy standing in the kitchen with his hands behind me?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: And I said, be a good looking girl if he ever came to you. On to him. Because he didn't say nothing. He just stood there and grinned. Watched the rest of us have a good time. But oh God, he was good looking. He's lost it now. But Frank said, that's Jesse Spencer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Don't you know him?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: And I said, no. Never did see him before. I know him. And I forgot all about him going to school up there when we was all on the mountain. Well, he was too young when I went to that school up there to go. Well, now he wasn't very young. If you stayed with grandma and walked that far, I wasn't either. You were about nine or ten years old. You wasn't in school then. Well, I went up there. I didn't go very long as I built the Spring Valley Schoolhouse. And went there then. Went from my grandmother's place down there. I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what happened then? Did Frank Rohn introduce you to him or what?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Yes, later on. And then he asked me to go home and take me home that night. And Frank told me to go ahead.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: What year was you up there?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Now, we were still on the homestead. You was on the homestead, but you wasn't going to school because you want to know them now. Well, yes, I went to school from the homestead. You went to school up there and the other school. I went to the Grandest Best School on top of the hill there. I did too. You went to Mrs. Blackburn. You went to the school there. And we walked from the homestead up there on that hill to school. You asked John. That's right. Where Lee Randall got him down that time and was tickling him and Andy Dyer pulled him off of him. If you had him, he'd tickle him to death. Right up on that schoolhouse platform. Didn't have any cloakroom or anything then up there. Just a great big porch out in front with no roof or nothing on it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you tell me, by the way, that you and Frank Rohn used to go together or do a dance when you didn't have anybody else to go with?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: All once in a while. He went to a basket social one time at the schoolhouse and he's bound he's going to get in my basket because he wanted my supper. That's why him and his brother Jack stayed at our house an awful lot when we lived down on the flat. So he bought my first old man, Vitito, an old widower. Got my basket and my brother told me before I went over there, he said, Mabel, I'll bet you ten dollars that Vitito gets your basket. And I said, if he does, I won't eat with him. He was an old Tennessee guy and his family was all grown up and gone and he was dirty. If he'd have been clean, I would eat with him. And he got my basket first and Frank went around and bought it to him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how did your brother, why did your brother think he was going to get it?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: He just told me that. And he told me before we left home, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And you're just guessing, eh?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Yeah.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Now I just made a plain round basket and put a big red rose right in the center of it. A white basket with a red rose in the center.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did that work now? You bid on the basket? Was that it?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Yeah. An auctioneer. They used to have them out there at school. I was quite often to raise money for the schools for different things. Say, a new blackboard or one time we had one and bought a desk for the school. The old one wore out and so we bought a new desk.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you remember about school?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: No.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: I don't remember too much about that. I was playing around with the plants. I went to Spring Valley School. I got the whole cart down. I left the ticket. I got the whole screw down.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: That's trouble. I used to draw maps. I draw a map there for the United States. Mrs. Green was a teacher. She gave me a hundred on that map. It was there in those schoolhouse for a good many years tacked up on the wall.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you like school when you was going?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Oh, I loved it. I started high school here in Troy. Walked from Randall Flathead. And I wanted to go to school so bad but I never had the fondue itch. My brother was born. My dad said I couldn't go to school. I'd have to take a home. So I did. This was when you had been starting in high school. Yeah. Well I was twenty years old. I had a time to start high school and I had been waiting to start.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You drink sugar or cream?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: No, I just take mine black. I love school. I used to stand on the stump up on the homestead. My mother would have to go help my dad to throw trees so he could work at the woods in the day time while we were in school. I used to stand on the stump and bowl because my mother wasn't there to comb my hair and braid. I had my own braid.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Comb your hair so before you went to school you mean?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Yes. Because we had to start early. We had to go two mile and a half and you had to start early.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you walk that way?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Yes, we walked. Well once in a while we'd get a ride back up home with the loggers. They were logging up in our neighborhood with jeans in and wide track wagons, logging wagons. We'd sometimes get a ride with one of them from the sawmill up but it was about a mile up the mountain from the school before we caught them and then just once in a while we'd catch them.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: I was going to ask Miss Chunk, she wanted to drown me but she left too quick. She wouldn't have anyway. She'd been afraid there'd been some lard in it. Why thank you. Because they're Adventists. They won't hardly eat anywhere. And I wouldn't lie to nobody about anything. Thank you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you start working around?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: You was probably pretty young when you started working at home. Well I helped Paul Lumber and Wood and stuff like that. I was a little kid.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Nine, ten years old, long in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you want some of your labor?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Well there was dogs. There were more dogs. They'd come out and then out of where to go. They'd go back in and get us up there by park I think it was somewhere.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Well he's a character, he looked pretty funny. He had kind of long hair. He's like the hippies do now.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: I guess his name was Davy Cohorn.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Davy who?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Cohorn. That's what his name was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Davy Cohorn?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Yeah.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: That's all I know. He had a name. They called him Wild Davy. Davy Cohorn.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there any reason why they called him Wild Davy?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Well I called him back up there in the woods I guess, so I know.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Here's that woodyard down here. They'd pile wood up down there.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Bill Justin used to buy all kinds of wood here. He'd load out cars to every day.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: He'd get put all over the country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did you, did you cut wood too? For that? Did you cut wood to Holland and Troy?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Yeah we cut wood every spring. We'd cut quite a bit of wood and they'd make a long rake of wood and haul it in in the wintertime. A hundred cord they made every year. We did too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A hundred cord just for the house?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: A hundred cord to keep our family going. That's all the way they had to make an eleven.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You sold it here at Troy then?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Yes. Well we didn't haul to Troy. His daddy did. But we hauled a howl up here. Loaded out. My daddy did. All of his wood and posts and ties that he had. He used to have more ships shipped out of this town here than any town between Lewiston and Spokane. I see them come in here with only two or three cars and have all they could take up from there with an engine from here up to something like that to come back and get the second bunch of cars to take it up the hill to haul.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Now they don't ship anywhere to nothing out here anymore.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well was it mostly wood they shipped?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Wood, ties, lumber, everything that way.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Used to be mills all around the country. They haul lumber in here and ship it out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the town a tough town like I've heard? Is that so that it was a pretty tough town in the early days?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Oh yeah, pretty rough.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Yeah, used to be quite a lot of Irish around here. They got a bunch of dagoes they called them. They came in here and they got in together and run them all town working on the railroads. They wouldn't come back in here for several years. They got through and they come back through here working. I guess they claimed they run them all town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just because they were dagoes?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: I guess that was it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this when they were building the road in 1890 or the potlatch railroad?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: No, the one going, the extra crews going down. They used to have crews that come in and riprap the road bank, the grade, the creek. They'd riprap the rock and that's what they were doing. I guess they run them out. I don't remember, that's what I heard them say.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the IWW? What have you heard about them?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Yeah, they used to have lots of things on the IWW that they didn't do. They were the ones that cleaned the log cabs up. They got the men getting decent bucks and things. They used to have bedbugs sticking flies and you couldn't sleep for them. They made them clean them up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there many wobbly's around Troy here?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: No, not too many I don't think.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: There was one guy coming here and I don't know where he was standing there or where he was. He came in here and robbed the bank. He got away with quite a bit of money and I was telling him to live right here next door. She was working in the bank when they robbed it. The detectives said if that guy worked his life they never would have called him. He came in here and he went and after he got his money like morning he went and bought a new car and paid for it home and things. Well they knew he didn't have that kind of money so they finally convicted him of it. Well if he said he worked his life they never would have called him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this in the early days or was this more recent?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: No, it was just years. It was quite a while ago but I don't know. It wasn't the early days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the, was there a lot of moonshining around here during the prohibition?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Oh yeah, I guess there was several of them made moonshining. I don't know. Well now you know one man out here maybe. I know a couple. I've heard Sam McKean made quite a bit. Well that's the main one right here in the town. He was out here just about a little over a mile from town.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: He lived up in the back up towards White Pine Mill. I guess he made quite a bit of moonshine. He never got caught either I heard. He's one of the guys that never did get caught. Yeah.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: He said he sat on a block of wood and talked to the officers and under the wood was his liquor. The block he was sitting on. So his daughter was sitting on a box out in the yard and they come in and she had a grudge over the box. Had a gallon jug under there and they looked all around and didn't find it. He was sitting on that so we go ahead and hunt around. He just sat there with a stick just like that and they hunt all around. And if they didn't find it, he used to have about some kind of one gallon jug. And they sharpened poles down towards the barn lot and he told them just move over. He took some of the chips where he had them covered up and took the five gallon jug.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The guy came out there and he had the jug buried in the shavings?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Yeah, he had the jug buried in the chips and what they said he had buried underneath there. They wouldn't look in the chip pile for a jug.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he just took it out and gave it to the guy?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Yeah, I told him to bring his jug back. He wanted that. Yeah, I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And when his daughter was sitting with a blanket over her lap?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Yeah, she was in a cell or something. She had the rug over the top of this couple box. Sitting on that and the jug getting underneath that.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Well he must have been a pretty careful guy because he never did get caught. Yeah. And they sat down there and there was a summer pea. It was a shirt. They'd sneak around and try and come in and catch him. And they claimed that, I don't know whether it was a sword or not, but they claimed he shot the booty off of a summer pea. And they talked to Adam Morelle. I don't know whether he did or not, but that's what the thing is. The fella told me about it. I heard Mrs. McKeen had a pretty good temper of her own if you'd tackled with her. Yeah, she was, there was nothing about anything over her. Yeah. I guess the little fella up here used to be on the telephone line. He was president and he was going to cut her telephone line off. And she saw a big clumpet and cut that pole and cut that telephone wire off so he'd never know how to get out. So he didn't, he said he didn't get up and cut the wire off. He left her alone. I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that supposed to be Gabriel Anderson?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Yeah. Yeah, I've heard that. Yeah, I guess she didn't get all the heat up for her own.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know Butcher Johnson?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Yeah. Yeah. Yeah, they were the only kids up here too.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Yeah, they lived here for years and years. You know, Butcher dropped, I don't know.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: I heard tell that he used to drive into town and she'd come walking into town after she got the chores done. Yeah, I guess it is. I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you first start driving team?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Oh, I was just a kid. I don't know how old I was. I wasn't very old.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you learn how to handle horses so good?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Oh, learning from experience.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: I could experiment and learn.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You tell me what's important about, what's the right way to handle a team?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Well, you got to pitch your collar and your hands up so it won't hurt your shoulders. And you got to have the people who don't know how to fit a collar on a horse. A horse's neck is narrow at the top and at the bottom. But you got to fill them in so that your collar fits all the way up and down the neck. You don't want to make a sore shoulder. And I had to learn all that and work in it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How do you fill these collars in? What do you fill them in with?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Oh, I fill them in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You fill them?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: If a collar is too big, I can put a pad on them. But lots of people that didn't have pads on them, they just had a collar. Well, if you had a, you can buckle them up at the top together so the collar fits your horse. You never have a sore shoulder.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Yeah. Well now, you were telling me that you run into lots of times where people weren't taking good care of their horses. Yeah.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: I worked horses and tried to fit the collar up on them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why was it you used on their shoulders? Axle grease?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: I tried to use the axle grease and the toe polish.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Not the polish, the soot wasn't it?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: That's the axle grease and the brick toe polish and they cut it off. Oh, oh, that got me. They made a salve out of it. Stir that in, they. Axle grease.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did that do? Healed them up?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Healed them, screw them, make them touch skin.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would you say would be some of the best teams that he had? Were there any teams he had that were real outstanding?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Well, he had two of them. He raised one team. Where Doc and Pet own brother and sister, Daddy, he raised them. They were a span of graze. And then he had a strata team, they were big, but boy they could go. And he didn't raise them, he bought them I guess. And bought them up there in the big bin country. And there was two special teams that we kept until they all died. We both loved them teams and drove them everywhere and on anything. And every time you'd tell that great team to go, boy they went and they could pull the biggest load for a little team. They weren't very big, not real big, but they were really strong and pullers. I worked them on a Durek fork. And they never stick a Durek fork in to what they'd take the load of. And they'd go by their self without a driver. When it tripped they'd back up. Cause I seen them do it right up there when we harvested together. And the horse lived the longest. We kept him until Clyde was three or four years old. And Dad would gather sweet corn from off of the garden up on the hill and bring it down and I'd cut it off and I fed him until he died. Cause he didn't have a tooth in his head. Yeah, yeah. He got that phosphorous. Yeah, that's what killed him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Phosphorous. Squirrel poison. Squirrel poison. It just beat him up. He'd eat the whole life through him. Shepherds. Putting that off for squirrels. And he'd throw it out in the grass. He'd eat the grass and eat some of them kernels of wheat or oats.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who was putting it out for the squirrels? Who was?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Shepherds. Our neighbor. Our neighbor.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well you said that your father thought real highly of his skill as a teamster?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Yes sir. He said he was one of the best teamsters he ever saw pick up a line. My daddy was a teamster. He drove stagecoach in California in early days. And he cowboyed down there for three or four years on the plains. And he was a real teamster. But he said, just get out. Do him. People that drove three horses, they couldn't even up the team. And he made a three horse evener that would even them up, you know. And they'd all pull the same. And nobody would ever believe that until they seen it. And seen him work them. Three horses.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did he do it?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Well he just made them. I don't know whether he took a piece of hardwood or a two before or what he made them out of. You know, the shorter shimel was about six horse hitch. And you got a horse in the fur on the wheel. He pulled against three in the lead. And the other two pulled against them four. And you had your hitch right back to the plow. And you took this like a three horse evener and you put that on there with your clevis right towards your plow. And this other horse, he had three, three fourths of length. And then three in that. And they had one fourth. One fourth. Well that just made a hitch. And whoever horse pulled the same. It was the only hitch that they ever seen. And you could take a little horse and a big one. One of them wouldn't be traveling up with the other one. And he'd fix the checks on them horses until they all evened up. And I'll take that great team I had. The mayor, she was tender mowless. The horse he was talking about. Well I had to give that mayor about eight inches more on that line to get him to walk even.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: To consider having a lot of people had the idea this was going out there. The line had a bunch of holes in it. You had to have them in the same hole. Well that's why they didn't know how to just have a horse. They wondered how I had more horses before they walked even. I had them until they pulled even all the time. That's just the line. He never would drive with a whip. I used to get so mad at him because he wouldn't drive with a whip. But he'd take the end of the line and he'd just cut the hair off a horse with the end of the line.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Same way with a quirt or a black snake or anything. Well our boy was good too. He took it after his dad though. But he practiced. I held a cigarette out and he cut it in three pieces with a black snake. With a rubber, not a rubber, leather hopper on it. My lord. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How come you didn't want to use a whip?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: If you're going to have a horse you got a whipping on them, you ain't no horseman. I don't care who he is. He told everyone and his daddy always drove with a whip. He told every one of them that had a whip that there wasn't no horseman. If you can't learn a horse to get up and go without a whipping, well you better quit.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: That's like going down the path of falling in the rough spot up there. Had a six horse team. He had a horse. He was a young horse, probably five, six years old. Something on this. He was kind of lazy. And he had him far away. And he'd be right back. The whole time he'd be back behind. And they'd keep pecking on him. Going tricks, tricks, tricks, tricks. I went over there with the plowing farm. And so I took the breast strap off and I had a piece of lace leather about that long. I took my knife and drilled a hole in that and the breast strap was long enough. I took that and put that lace leather on the end of that. I just reached out and never said a word to him. When he got back I just reached out with that strap and hit him on the leg. Well, he got up there and got up there pretty long. I never said a word to him. He come back, I just reached out and tipped him a little more with that leather.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: And I had that horse walking up there where he blogged. I wasn't hollering tricks at him at all. One time I told him to go and I started him out. And finally, I got out of there for five days and go along there. And then this horse was walking up where he blogged. So Harry Mitchell, he come along and he was riding on the saddle horse. He was the one that owned the horses. And going along and the horse was walking like he ought to. He said, I'd like to know what you've done. He said, well, tricks, you've got him walking where he blogged. Well, I just fixed it. And I took the strap off where I was hanging on the lever. And here I just reached out and clipped him with that. I said, I don't holler at a horse. If I can't let him go without hollering at him, I said, I don't learn anything. So I showed him. I don't know how he made it afterwards. I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever run into horses that wouldn't even move? Did you ever run into horses that wouldn't go at all? That wouldn't move? Oh, Bocky?

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Yes, I had him Bocky. He'd come around that too without a whip. He'd come around the fella because they wasn't telling the right. And he didn't have the collar to fit up on and all them things. And the horse, you know, if he's pulling, that collar is going to hurt his shoulders. He ain't going to pull them, that's the thing. Just like you are. If you were going to carry something on your shoulder, you'd wind up here where you could... You know, if you had it on you and it pulled on your shoulder, it would hurt. You wouldn't lift against us. You wouldn't have to get it on your shoulder. Same way with a horse.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: When we first got into the country here, well, he had to do whatever he worked hard on. The first combines was pulled with 32 head horses. We did have a picture of some of them, but I don't know where they got off to. Out south of Moscow on the snow place we had one. 32 head of horses. Well, they couldn't drive them with a whip. They had rocks. Some used rocks. I wouldn't stand around like that at all. They'd throw rocks and burn them to get them to go.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
words: Well, I wasn't... Well, you couldn't reach. Out 32 head of horses with a whip. The seat set away up. The horses backing underneath you. The seat set up ahead there.

speaker: Jesse and Mabel Spencer
